subsystem: rsyslog runtime C-string / dynamic string-buffer class (rsCStr / cstr_t). This is the growth routine rsCStrExtendBuf() that reallocates the backing byte buffer of a heap-allocated, length-tracked string object so it can hold at least iMinNeeded bytes. It is the single choke point every append/concat path in the string class funnels through when the current buffer is too small.
provenance: Identified from rsyslog-specific naming and idioms: the type cstr_t and function name rsCStrExtendBuf, the growth constant RS_STRINGBUF_ALLOC_INCREMENT, the uchar typedef, and the rsyslog error-handling macros DEFiRet / CHKmalloc / finalize_it: / RETiRet are the signature of rsyslog's runtime/stringbuf.c. The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_3425_vuln' marks it as a PrimeVul-sourced C sample; the file name carries the CWE-189 (numeric errors) family label.
trust_boundary: iMinNeeded is a size_t chosen by the caller and ultimately derives from the length of data being appended to the string object. In rsyslog, cstr_t buffers accumulate log message content, parsed template fields, property values, and network-sourced (TCP/UDP/RELP/imfile) text — all attacker-influenceable. An adversary who can make a string grow past ~64 KiB controls the high bits of iMinNeeded that this routine silently discards. The struct fields pThis->iBufSize and pThis->pBuf are the trusted state this function is responsible for keeping consistent.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function grows the string object *pThis so its backing buffer can hold at least iMinNeeded bytes. iMinNeeded is a size_t, i.e. a full machine-word unsigned count (up to 2^32-1 or 2^64-1). pNewBuf is a temporary that will receive the realloc() result. Critically, iNewSize — the variable that holds the computed new capacity for the entire routine — is declared unsigned short, a 16-bit type whose maximum value is 65535. DEFiRet is rsyslog's macro that declares the local rsRetVal iRet return-status variable used by the CHKmalloc/finalize_it/RETiRet error protocol.

This is the large-request branch: when the requested minimum exceeds one allocation increment, the code rounds iMinNeeded up to the next whole multiple of RS_STRINGBUF_ALLOC_INCREMENT (a compile-time growth granularity, e.g. 128) and adds one extra increment of slack. The arithmetic (iMinNeeded / INCREMENT + 1) * INCREMENT is performed on the size_t operand iMinNeeded so the multiplication itself is computed in size_t width, but the RESULT is then assigned into the 16-bit unsigned short iNewSize, truncating it modulo 65536. The in-code comment's insistence that 'all of this are integer operations' is exactly the trap: the author reasoned about integer division/rounding but not about the destination type's width.

The small-request branch (iMinNeeded within one increment): grow the current capacity pThis->iBufSize by exactly one increment. pThis->iBufSize is itself the buffer's stored capacity field; combined with the +INCREMENT this is the ordinary geometric-ish growth step. The sum is again assigned into the 16-bit iNewSize.

Regardless of branch, the current buffer size pThis->iBufSize is added on top so the new buffer holds the existing contents plus the newly requested room. Note this means in the large branch the final capacity is roughly (rounded iMinNeeded) + current size, and in the small branch it is (current + increment) + current. All of this accumulates into the 16-bit iNewSize.

The realloc grows (or shrinks) the existing pThis->pBuf allocation to iNewSize * sizeof(uchar) bytes (sizeof(uchar) == 1). The result is captured into the temporary pNewBuf first — this is the CORRECT realloc idiom, since on failure the original pThis->pBuf is not lost. CHKmalloc wraps the assignment: if the returned pointer is NULL it sets iRet to RS_RET_OUT_OF_MEMORY and jumps to finalize_it, so a NULL dereference on allocation failure is guarded. Only on success are the trusted fields committed: pThis->iBufSize records the new capacity and pThis->pBuf adopts the new block. The capacity written back is the possibly-truncated 16-bit iNewSize.

Standard rsyslog epilogue: finalize_it: is the goto target used by CHKmalloc on failure, and RETiRet returns the accumulated iRet status to the caller. On the failure path pThis is left unchanged (old buffer and old iBufSize intact), which is correct; on success the caller assumes pThis->pBuf now has iBufSize bytes available.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The capacity variable iNewSize is a 16-bit unsigned short but iMinNeeded is a size_t. When a caller needs 65536 or more bytes (a >64 KiB log line, imported file record, or accumulated template/property value — all attacker-influenceable), the size_t rounding result at line 16 is truncated modulo 65536 when stored into iNewSize. Concretely, iMinNeeded == 65536 rounds to 65664 which truncates to 128; iMinNeeded just under a multiple of 65536 can truncate to a tiny value or even 0. The routine then reports SUCCESS while the buffer is far smaller than the caller asked for. The caller, trusting that pThis now holds iMinNeeded bytes, copies its full-size payload into pThis->pBuf and writes past the end of the undersized heap allocation — a remote heap buffer overflow (CWE-787) driven by an integer truncation (CWE-190/CWE-191). The invariant the implementation must uphold is that the computed capacity variable is at least as wide as the size_t request AND is verified to be >= iMinNeeded before it is accepted; the fix is to make iNewSize a size_t (this is exactly the difference between the vulnerable and fixed rsyslog versions).

Even without an oversized single request, the additions accumulate into the 16-bit iNewSize and can wrap. The small branch computes pThis->iBufSize + INCREMENT and then line 20 adds pThis->iBufSize AGAIN, so a buffer already grown to near 32 KiB produces a sum exceeding 65535 that wraps to a small number. This lets a string that has legitimately grown over many appends suddenly reallocate SMALLER than its current contents, then have pThis->iBufSize recorded as that small wrapped value at line 24 — corrupting the length/capacity invariant and setting up subsequent out-of-bounds writes. The implementation must perform the capacity arithmetic in a width that cannot overflow for any reachable buffer size and reject/abort if the accumulation exceeds SIZE limits.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
CHKmalloc(pNewBuf = (uchar*) realloc(pThis->pBuf, iNewSize * sizeof(uchar)));
	pThis->iBufSize = iNewSize;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: When the truncation/wrap yields iNewSize == 0, realloc(pThis->pBuf, 0) is implementation-defined: it may free the block and return NULL (which CHKmalloc treats as failure, leaving stale state) or return a minimum-size non-NULL pointer. In the non-NULL-return case CHKmalloc passes, pThis->iBufSize is set to 0 and pThis->pBuf points at a zero/near-zero-length block, so the very next byte the caller writes overflows. This is the downstream consequence of the width bug and confirms that the capacity actually stored (line 24) is attacker-controllable to arbitrarily small values. The guard needed is an explicit 'iNewSize must be >= iMinNeeded and > 0 after computation' check before the realloc.

summary: This rsyslog string-buffer growth routine computes and stores the new capacity in a 16-bit unsigned short (iNewSize) while its request parameter iMinNeeded is a full-width size_t. For any request at/over 64 KiB, or once accumulated buffer growth passes 65535, the capacity truncates/wraps modulo 65536, so the routine reallocates a buffer far smaller than requested yet returns success — a classic integer-overflow-to-heap-overflow primitive (CWE-190 -> CWE-787). The realloc NULL handling and temp-pointer idiom are correct; the bug is entirely the undersized capacity type.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Contract mismatch: a size_t request is reduced through a 16-bit capacity variable; the signature promises to satisfy iMinNeeded but the body cannot for large values.
Primary defect: 16-bit capacity variable truncates the size_t-derived new size.
Temporary receiving the realloc result; used correctly (assigned before overwriting pThis->pBuf), carries no independent contract.
Standard rsyslog status-return macro; no security contract of its own here.
Attacker-influenceable request magnitude whose high bits are silently discarded by the 16-bit destination.
Compile-time growth granularity constant; the rounding math is arithmetically sound — the hazard is the destination width, not this constant.
size_t-width rounding result assigned into 16-bit iNewSize: truncation modulo 65536.
Accumulation into 16-bit iNewSize can wrap once buffer growth exceeds 65535, producing a smaller-than-current capacity.
realloc idiom is correct (temp pointer) and NULL is checked; the size argument is the problem, already captured by the iNewSize finding. realloc(...,0) edge is covered as a consequence.
Capacity field written back from the truncated/wrapped iNewSize, corrupting the object's size invariant for all later accesses.
Backing-buffer pointer; updated correctly only on realloc success. Its danger is the undersized allocation, captured by the iNewSize finding.
Error epilogue; leaves pThis unchanged on failure, which is correct.

findings.title: New buffer capacity computed/stored in 16-bit unsigned short truncates a size_t request (integer overflow -> heap buffer overflow)
findings.bug_class: Integer truncation of size computation leading to undersized allocation and heap buffer overflow
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0036_cwe189.c (rsCStrExtendBuf), with the overflowing write in the caller/append path — implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The variable holding the computed new capacity must be at least as wide as size_t (the type of iMinNeeded) and the routine must guarantee the final capacity is >= iMinNeeded before returning success; capacity arithmetic must not truncate or wrap for any reachable request size.
findings.failure_mode: A caller requests iMinNeeded >= 65536 bytes (e.g. a >64 KiB log line / imfile record / accumulated property). Line 16 computes the rounded size in size_t width but assigns it into the 16-bit iNewSize, truncating modulo 65536 (e.g. 65536 -> 128, or a value that truncates to 0). realloc then allocates the tiny truncated size, pThis->iBufSize is set to it, and the function returns RS_RET_OK. The caller, trusting the buffer now holds iMinNeeded bytes, copies its full payload and writes past the end of the heap block — remote heap buffer overflow, memory corruption, potential RCE.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm iNewSize's type (unsigned short here) versus the fixed rsyslog version's size_t, and confirm no post-computation check verifies iNewSize >= iMinNeeded; trace a caller that copies iMinNeeded bytes into pThis->pBuf after this returns.

findings.title: Repeated additions of iBufSize accumulate into 16-bit iNewSize and can wrap to a smaller-than-current capacity
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer overflow (wrap) in capacity accumulation
findings.invariant: The running capacity accumulation must be computed in a width that cannot wrap for any buffer that has already been allocated, and the result must be monotonically >= the previous pThis->iBufSize.
findings.failure_mode: After a string has grown to near 32-64 KiB, the small branch (line 18) plus line 20 add pThis->iBufSize into the 16-bit iNewSize twice; the sum exceeds 65535 and wraps to a small value. realloc then SHRINKS the buffer below its current contents and pThis->iBufSize (line 24) records the wrapped small capacity, so the object now claims less space than the data it already holds — every subsequent append writes out of bounds. Reachable purely through incremental appends without any single oversized request.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the type of pThis->iBufSize in cstr_t and that no saturation/overflow check guards line 20; verify behavior when pThis->iBufSize is already > 32768.

findings.title: Truncated capacity of zero yields realloc(ptr,0) and a zero-length buffer recorded as valid
findings.code:
CHKmalloc(pNewBuf = (uchar*) realloc(pThis->pBuf, iNewSize * sizeof(uchar)));
	pThis->iBufSize = iNewSize;
findings.bug_class: Zero/near-zero allocation from wrapped size accepted as success
findings.invariant: The routine must reject a computed capacity of 0 (or any value < iMinNeeded) before calling realloc, so that success always implies a usable buffer of at least the requested size.
findings.failure_mode: When the truncation/wrap makes iNewSize == 0, realloc(pThis->pBuf, 0) may return a non-NULL minimal pointer; CHKmalloc passes, pThis->iBufSize becomes 0 and pThis->pBuf points at a zero-length block, so the caller's first write overflows immediately. This is the extreme case of the width bug and shows success is returned for an unusable buffer.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm platform realloc(ptr,0) semantics and that there is no guard rejecting iNewSize==0 before line 23; confirm the failure path leaves stale pBuf if it returns NULL.

audit_checklist: Change iNewSize (line 5) to size_t (or size_t-wide) and re-audit every assignment at lines 16, 18, 20 for any remaining narrowing.
After computing the new capacity, assert/verify iNewSize >= iMinNeeded and iNewSize > 0 BEFORE calling realloc; return an error if not.
Verify the type/width of cstr_t.iBufSize and ensure line 20's accumulation cannot wrap for the maximum buffer the application will grow.
Trace all callers of rsCStrExtendBuf (append/concat/setlen paths) and confirm none copy iMinNeeded bytes into pThis->pBuf without re-checking the actual returned capacity.
Guard the multiplication iNewSize * sizeof(uchar) against overflow once iNewSize is widened (and cap the maximum allowed buffer size).
Confirm realloc(ptr,0) is never reached with a live object, and that the CHKmalloc failure path preserves the old buffer and old iBufSize.
